thai: ข้าวเนื้อตุ๋นเอ็น

food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dishes with braised meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai braised beef brisket and tendon rice. FOODON_00002473 (beef dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Beef, cooked) used as closest parent. No specific code exists for braised beef brisket and tendon rice composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Thai braised beef and rice dish.

allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and oyster sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 150 g jasmine rice, 120 g braised beef brisket, 60 g beef tendon, and 70 g braising sauce and garnish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, brisket, flat half, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, braised (FDC ID 174032) and Cooked white rice, long-grain (FDC ID 169756); composite estimate weighted by serving proportion
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai braised beef brisket and tendon rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of: (1) USDA FDC braised beef brisket (FDC ID 174032) at ~45% of serving weight, (2) USDA FDC cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756) at ~37.5% of serving weight, and (3) beef tendon (collagen-rich connective tissue, high protein, low fat, estimated from USDA FDC beef tendon cooked FDC ID 169544) at ~15% of serving weight, with braising sauce contributing residual sodium and sugar. Per-100g values are derived from this composite. Confidence is medium due to estimation approach.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice (jasmine-type, cooked) GI ~64–72; composite dish GI anchored to jasmine rice component as dominant glycemic driver
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai braised beef brisket and tendon rice as a composite dish. GI is estimated at 64 based on jasmine white rice (GI 64–72, University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source. Beef brisket and tendon contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (64 × 66.0) / 100 = 42.2, rounded to 43.6 accounting for sauce sugars. gi_category set to medium (GI 55–69).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from braising sauce (soy sauce, oyster sauce)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single 400 g serving provides approximately 1520 mg sodium, representing 66% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by jasmine white rice; moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the jasmine rice component contributes a medium GI of approximately 64 and a glycemic load of ~43 per serving; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef brisket and elevated sodium from braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (~6 g per serving) from beef brisket and high sodium (~1520 mg per serving) from the braising sauce warrants moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, particularly those with concurrent dyslipidaemia or hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Brisket and Tendon Rice (Thai: ข้าวเนื้อตุ๋นเอ็น, romanized: Khao Nuea Tun En) is a Thai braised beef dish served over steamed jasmine rice. The dish features two distinct cuts: beef brisket, a flavourful and moderately fatty cut from the lower chest of the animal, and beef tendon, a collagen-rich connective tissue that becomes gelatinous and tender after prolonged slow braising. Both are braised together in a savoury sauce typically composed of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, star anise, five-spice powder, and palm sugar, producing a deeply umami, slightly sweet, and aromatic braising liquid. The dish is served over fragrant long-grain jasmine rice, which absorbs the braising sauce. Texturally, the brisket is soft and yielding, the tendon is silky and gelatinous, and the rice is fluffy and fragrant. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 592 kcal, 43 g protein, 17 g fat, and 66 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium due to the soy-based braising sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The jasmine rice component drives a medium glycemic index (~64), relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The beef tendon is notably rich in collagen and glycine. This dish is commonly found in Thai restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often listed as a rice plate or braised beef rice bowl.

jasmine rice: ข้าวหอมมะลิ

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai braised beef brisket and tendon rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central entries for braised beef brisket, cooked jasmine-type white rice, and beef tendon. GI is estimated from jasmine rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct dish-level measurement in any primary approved source.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this composite estimation. All other -1 values in per_100g are sentinel values indicating the nutrient was not reported in the source data for the component foods used in this composite estimate. Nutrient values are composite estimates weighted by serving proportion (beef brisket ~45%, jasmine rice ~37.5%, beef tendon ~15%, sauce ~2.5%). Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and oyster sauce in the braising liquid. The dish is not confirmed halal as it is served at a Thai restaurant and halal certification status is not verified. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (64 × 66.0 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 ≈ 42.2, adjusted to 43.6 to account for residual sugars from braising sauce.
